result:
If I could choose one superpower, it wouldn’t be flight or invisibility. It would be the ability to perfectly recall a book after reading it just once. My shelves groan under the weight of stories I’ve loved and lost—not the plots, but the specific texture of the prose, the exact turn of phrase that made me catch my breath. I remember the feeling of a novel, its emotional weather, but the details slip away like sand through a sieve. I want to hold them all at once: the crisp dialogue of a noir mystery, the sprawling, fragrant descriptions of a historical epic, the devastatingly simple line from a poem that explained my own heart to me.

There’s a quiet magic in the act of re-reading, of course—discovering new layers, meeting the old words as a slightly different person. But I am greedy. I want to build a palace in my mind where every room is a different book, fully furnished, waiting for me to wander through. I could stand in the shadow of Moby Dick’s whale, then turn a corner and smell the biscuits baking in *Pride and Prejudice*’s Longbourn kitchen, all within a single thought.

Perhaps this desire is rooted in a fear of time passing, of forgetting. Books are conversations with the greatest minds and imaginations across centuries. To forget the words feels like losing the friend. So I keep collecting, a magpie for beautiful sentences, hoping that even if the specifics fade, their shape has altered me. The books we love become the architecture of our inner world, even if we can’t quote them chapter and verse. They are the quiet, permanent residents of our souls.

Perhaps this desire is rooted in a fear of time passing, of forgetting. Books are conversations with the greatest minds and imaginations across centuries. To forget the words feels like losing the friend. So I keep collecting, a magpie for beautiful sentences, hoping that even if the specifics fade, their shape has altered me. prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
